data.title: U.K.'s Rising Debt Costs And Fiscal Uncertainty
data.description:
By Krzysztof Kamiński

The British economy is facing serious fiscal challenges that are increasing tensions in the bond market and may affect the pound’s exchange rate. Despite the government’s declarations about maintaining budget balance, experts warn that the tax increases announced by Chancellor Rachel Reeves are too dispersed to meaningfully strengthen public finances and ease pressure on debt markets.

A prospect of 6% yields

David Zahn of Franklin Templeton forecasts that the yield on 30-year UK government bonds could reach 6%, highlighting the government’s difficulties in financing rising expenditures.

The current spread between UK and German bonds of the same maturity stands at 176 basis points and 78 basis points relative to France, placing the UK among the most expensive developed countries in which to borrow.

Zahn, who sold all his UK bonds back in March, believes that only a further rise in debt-servicing costs will force the government into genuine fiscal adjustment. As he puts it, “Eventually yields will be so high that the government won’t be able to sweep the problem under the carpet any longer.”

Questionable revenue and political risk

Although Reeves has pledged to increase the “fiscal buffer” and avoided the most controversial tax hikes (such as taxes on pension withdrawals or capital gains), rating agencies and think tanks doubt the long-term stability of the plan.

November also saw the second-worst outflow from UK equity funds in history; investors withdrew £3 billion, reflecting market tensions ahead of the budget announcement. Only after the budget was published did the outflows stop, suggesting investors feared radical changes that ultimately were not introduced.

GBP: Short-term relief, long-term risks

The pound has recently appreciated against a weakening dollar, and the bond market calmed after the budget release. However, by the end of the European session, GBP/USD met resistance at the 200-period SMA, which also aligns with the 50% Fibonacci retracement of the decline seen between September and November this year. Structural issues within the UK economy - high debt levels, low growth, and weak investment sentiment - remain unresolved.

Meanwhile, investors continue to avoid UK funds - only one month out of the last 55 saw a net inflow of capital. The government is attempting to stimulate the market through measures such as tax incentives for new listed companies and changes to the ISA system to encourage equity investment.

Strong commodities and decorrelation in the energy market

Against the backdrop of fiscal problems, the commodities sector remains the one bright spot in the UK market - copper and gold prices are hitting records, supporting mining companies’ valuations. The FTSE 100 owes much of its strength to the rising weight of mining stocks in the index.

The energy sector (FTSE 350 Energy) has also seen gains recently despite falling oil prices, likely driven by high dividends and share buyback programmes. However, investors should be aware that valuations may not hold if the geopolitical situation improves - for instance, if peace is achieved in Ukraine. On Friday, the index recorded losses of more than 1.76%.

The UK is at a turning point. A relatively strong currency and buoyant commodities sector contrast sharply with deep concerns over the stability of public finances.

The prospect of rising bond yields and tax uncertainty may, in the coming months, once again increase the government’s financing costs and weaken investor confidence in the pound and UK assets.

Original Post

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: December Kicks Off With 3 Notable Launches In A Week With 7 SPACs
data.description:
The US IPO market entered the final stretch of the year with a few small listings, a wave of SPACs, and three notable deal launches on the calendar for the week ahead (CDNL, LMRI, WLTH). AI developer Anthropic, last valued at $183 billion, sent shockwaves when news broke that it hired a law firm to help prep for a possible IPO late next year.

Three micro-cap IPO pricings

Dental services provider Park Dental Partners (PARK) priced its IPO at the midpoint to raise $20 million at an $87 million market cap. The diversified dental services network partners with dentists and others to provide oral surgery, periodontics, pediatric dentistry, prosthodontics, and other services in the Midwest. Park Dental Partners finished the week up 2%.

Singapore-based flooring materials distributor SMJ International Holdings (SMJF) priced its US IPO at $4.00 to raise $10 million at a $110 million market cap. The company sells and distributes carpet tiles, broadloom carpets, vinyl tiles, and other flooring projects in Singapore and 20 other countries, including Malaysia, Indonesia, and the Philippines. SMJ ended the week up 10%.

Israeli hydrogel implant developer Regentis Biomaterials (RGNT) also listed in the US this past week, raising $10 million at a $47 million market cap, as well as ginger-based soft drink maker Reed’s (OTCQX:REED), which completed its NYSE American uplisting, raising $10 million at a $46 million market cap; these valuations were too small to be included in our 2025 IPO stats, and are therefore excluded in the table below.

Seven new SPACs raise a combined $1.5 billion

New America Acquisition I (NWAXU) raised $300 million to target US tech, healthcare, and logistics companies; the SPAC is led by Kevin McGurn (T-Mobile, Vevo) and features advisory board members Donald Trump Jr. and Eric Trump. SilverBox Capital’s SilverBox V (SBXE.U) raised $240 million. Bitcoin Infrastructure Acquisition (BIXIU) raised $200 million, targeting digital assets. Maritime-focused General Purpose Acquisition (GPACU) raised $200 million. Activate Energy Acquisition (AEAQU) raised $200 million to target oil and gas. Safeguard Acquisition (SAC.U) raised $200 million to target aerospace and security. Leapfrog Acquisition (LFACU) raised $125 million to target energy and infrastructure outside of the US.

9 IPOs During the Week of December 1st, 2025

Blank check company targeting the digital asset industry.

Eight SPACs submitted initial filings. Avi Katz’s GigCapital9 (GIXXU) filed to raise $220 million. Art Technology Acquisition filed to raise $220 million, targeting tech and art. Hennessy Capital Investment VIII (HCICU) filed to raise $175 million, targeting industrials. Muzero Acquisition (MUZRU.RC) filed to raise $175 million, targeting technology-enabled businesses. Blue Water Acquisition IV filed to raise $125 million to target healthcare and tech. United Acquisition I (UACU) filed to raise $100 million, led by Globis Capital Advisors’ Paul Packer. Mammon Omicron Acquisition (OMICU.RC) filed to raise $60 million, led by Chinese executives. Also formed by Chinese executives, APEX Tech Acquisition (TRADU) filed to raise $60 million to target US companies.

Week Ahead

The 2025 IPO window is quickly narrowing, with just two weeks left in the year's typical IPO calendar. Three sizable offerings are scheduled for the week ahead, featuring fintech, healthcare, and infrastructure. With markets recovering from a recent sell-off in growth stocks, we expect several more launches from the pipeline to tee up listings for mid-December, including a potential mega IPO from medical supplies giant Medline (MDLN).

VC-backed robo-advisor Wealthfront (WLTH), raising $450 million at a $2.4 billion market cap, is likely next week's best-known deal. The automated investing platform serves more than 1.3 million clients with over $88 billion in platform assets, offering both investment advisory and cash management. First launched in 2011, its platform assets skyrocketed over the past three years as higher interest rates drove demand for cash management. The company highlights its Millennial and Gen Z customer base, asset retention rate, and profitability (39% operating margin). However, growth has decelerated recently, while cash spreads could be pressured by rate cuts. Cornerstones BlackRock and Wellington plan to take down one-third of the deal.

Backed by PE firm Welsh Carson, Lumexa Imaging (LMRI) is raising $463 million at a $1.8 billion market cap. Formerly known as US Radiology Specialists, the company operates 184 outpatient diagnostic imaging centers in the US, primarily in Texas and the Southeast, which provide MRIs and CT scans as well as mammograms, ultrasounds, and X-rays. The company has expanded significantly through acquisitions as well as opening de novo centers.

Cardinal Infrastructure (CDNL) aims to raise $242 million at a $769 million market cap. The largely executive-owned company provides wet utility installations (water, sewer, and stormwater systems) for buildings, along with related services. While it has significant exposure to the housing market in North Carolina (particularly Raleigh), that market has served it well so far, boasting strong growth and leading margins.

Several lock-ups are expiring in the week ahead, including four from companies that raised at least $50 million.

IPO Market Snapshot

The Renaissance IPO Indices are market cap-weighted baskets of newly public companies. As of 12/4/2025, the Renaissance IPO Index was up 7.7% year-to-date, while the S&P 500 was up 18.0%. Renaissance Capital’s IPO ETF (IPO) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Kenvue (KVUE) and Reddit (RDDT). The Renaissance International IPO Index was up 36.9% year-to-date, while the ACWX was up 30.6%. Renaissance Capital’s International IPO ETF (IPOS) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Galderma and Horizon Robotics.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 12/06/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

data.title: Markets Cool In November: Real Estate Takes The Lead
data.description:
November 2025 saw modest movements across most asset classes, with U.S. real estate staging a notable comeback, gaining +2.37% after October’s decline. Despite the monthly gain, U.S. real estate remains up 5.75% for the year.

Most asset classes posted small positive returns for the month. Bonds gained +0.60%, pushing their year-to-date return to 7.43%. Commodities added +0.26%, bringing their 2025 gain to 6.89%.

Managed Futures continued its recovery with a +0.24% increase, marking its fourth consecutive positive month, though still down -1.19% for the year. World stocks and U.S. stocks showed similar modest gains of 0.21% and 0.20%, respectively, with year-to-date returns of 29.28% and 17.62%.

Hedge funds experienced a slight decline of -0.10%, their first negative month since March, yet maintained a solid 7.70% return for 2025. Cash continued its reliable performance with a +0.32% gain, bringing its year-to-date return to 3.90%.

As the final month of 2025 closes, world stocks maintain their substantial lead in year-to-date performance, while most asset classes remain comfortably positive for the year.

Past performance is not indicative of future results.

Past performance is not indicative of future results.

Sources: Managed Futures = SocGen CTA Index

Cash - US T-Bill 13-week coupon equivalent annual rate/12, with YTD the sum of each month’s value

Bonds - Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND)

Hedge funds - IQ Hedge Multi-Strategy Tracker ETF (QAI)

Commodities - iShares S&P GSCI Commodity-Indexed Trust ETF (GSG)

Real Estate - iShares U.S. Real Estate ETF (IYR)

World stocks - iShares MSCI ACWI ex-U.S. ETF (ACWX)

US stocks - SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY)

All ETF performance data from YCharts

data.title: MBB: Take Advantage Of This Infrequent Opportunity
data.description:
Thesis

Mortgages are not usually exciting. Agency mortgages even less. But once every decade, when rates are set to move lower aggressively, the space can become interesting. One of the largest ETFs in the MBS space is the iShares MBS ETF (MBB), which sports a $39 billion AUM (assets under management).

In today's article we are going to go through MBB's structure and composition and outline why we believe the fund to be a robust investment for 2026.

Fund composition - Agency MBS only

Let us start with the fund objective as present on its website:

The iShares MBS ETF seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of investment-grade mortgage-backed pass-through securities issued and/or guaranteed by U.S. government agencies.

The respective index is the Bloomberg US MBS Index, thus making MBB a passive ETF. The current fund composition is as follows:

  • Federal National Mortgage Association: 44.6%
  • Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corporation: 29%
  • Government National Mortgage Association: 24%
  • Uniform MBS: 1.2%
  • Cash: residual

Since all the holdings are Agency MBS bonds, they hold the same rating as U.S. treasuries and do not have credit risk. Other analytics are as follows:

  • WAC: 3.5%
  • Duration: 5.4 years
  • OAS: 33 bps
  • Convexity: -0.5

There are over 11,000 holdings in the ETF, and the fund comes with a 30-day SEC yield of 4.15%. As we are writing this article, 5-year treasuries are trading at 3.7%; thus, the fund provides for a spread over treasuries given the MBS product.

Mortgages are boring

Because they are essentially treasuries plus a spread, mortgages with an intermediate duration are fairly boring:

  • 2018: 0.8%
  • 2019: 6.2%
  • 2020: 4.0%
  • 2021: -1.4%
  • 2022: -11.7%
  • 2023: 5.0%
  • 2024: 1.3%
  • 2025: 7.9%

If we look at the historic returns for MBB, we get a sense of the 'excitement' around mortgages. A staggering +0.8% in 2018 will not provide for a good party conversation. Nor will the 'astounding' +1.3% return in 2024. Intermediate MBS bonds do not tend to do much unless one comes from a high rates environment. Like today's macro picture. 'Boring' also equates to a very low volatility, with the annualized volatility as per the Seeking Alpha 'Risk' tab at only 5.2%.

Macro picture - new Fed Chair might cut aggressively

In the United States, the Federal Reserve is the central bank, and it is mandated to implement monetary policy without interference from the executive branch (i.e., the President). The general idea is for the Fed to implement a monetary policy that brings long term stability rather than short term political objectives.

However, the President is the one who nominates the Fed Chair every four years, which is then confirmed by the Senate. Jerome Powell's term is set to expire in May 2026, opening the door for the President to nominate a new Chair. As per the latest political observations, Kevin Hassett, the chairman of the National Economic Council and long-time Trump adviser, is set to be announced as the new Fed Chair.

The market's view is that Kevin Hassett might embark on an aggressive Fed Funds cutting exercise in order to achieve political goals:

Dec 3 (Reuters) - Bond investors have expressed concerns to the U.S. Treasury that Kevin Hassett could aggressively cut interest rates to align with President Donald Trump's preferences, the Financial Times reported on Wednesday, citing several people familiar with the conversations.

While the Fed controls the front end of the yield curve and the intermediate portion to a certain extent, the bond market controls the long end. As we saw above, MBB's duration sits at 5.4 years, thus falling in the intermediate bucket, and is thus very much correlated with what the Fed does. If we look historically, we can see a very high correlation between Fed Funds and 5-year rates:

The correlation is very high during times when rates move lower or higher. 5-year rates tend to 'anticipate' to a certain extent Fed Funds, and front-run them. Please note how 5-year rates started moving up during 2022 before the Fed changed the monetary stance. Similarly, in 2025, 5-year rates anticipated Fed cuts. Once Fed Funds stabilize, there is a bit of a term premium (i.e., spread) between Fed Funds and 5-year rates.

What is the expected return for 2026?

We have shown you above the close correlation between Fed Funds and 5-year rates. The ultimate net result for 2026 will be an equation to be solved by the next Fed chair. However, MBB is correctly placed to take advantage of a Chair that is very aggressive. Let us see a couple of cases.

Base Case: cutting Fed Funds to 2.5%

If the Fed ends up cutting Fed Funds to 2.5%, and assuming a term premium of 50 bps, we get 5-year rates down to 3%. This means a move from roughly 3.7% to 3%. Given the ETF's duration, this translates into a capital gain of roughly 3.8%, which, when added on top of the yield, can provide for a total return in excess of 7.5% in the next 12 months.

Investors can permutate around with the neutral Fed Funds rate and the term premium, but for a fund like MBB, a total return in excess of 7% is not something we see very often. In fact, in the past decade we have only seen it once, and that is this year (2025 is on track to achieve a total return in excess of 7%).

The policymakers have been fairly transparent about wanting lower rates in order to tackle a soaring deficit and high costs of the U.S. debt, and while they do not own the long end of the curve, they can certainly lower the front end. MBB is an ideal 'boring' instrument to take advantage of that dynamic, and the fund is set to be able to clock another year of +7% in total returns.

Conclusion

MBB is a passive ETF that holds Agency MBS with an intermediate duration. The name has a low volatility profile and does not run any credit risk. The main returns driver here is represented by rates. MBB does best when the Fed cuts aggressively, and the potential nomination of Hassett might achieve just that. Given its 5.4-year duration and 4.15% SEC yield. We are penciling in a total return in excess of 7% for 2026 in our base case.

data.title: Victoria's Secret: A Strong Business In A Challenging Sector
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Before the Q3 results presentation, Victoria's Secret (VSCO) had a 25% increase in its share price in November, recovering most of the decline seen in 2025. This optimistic perspective was consolidated with the very good sales of the quarter, which exceeded consensus estimates by $60 million, reaching $1.47 billion. The best result was the improvement in EPS compared to expectations: -$0.27 versus the consensus estimate of $-0.59.

Although the company's results are good, I'm assuming the market already discounted that performance, which drove the price up to 2025 highs. With the quarter results, I can see a consolidated and resilient business, but in a challenging environment for fashion, as I'll show later. Adding to that challenging market, with a P/E ratio above the sector average (22.4x vs. 19.6x), the burden of tariffs, and a weak forecast for increased consumption by 2026, I think it will be difficult to surpass its recent highs. But I want to start with the positive aspects of Q3 and the strengths of the business.

Q3 results exceeded expectations

Revenue was $1.47 billion, and it grew 9% YoY, while the gross profit grew even more: 15% YoY, due to the increase of the comparable stores sales. The net loss narrowed YoY from $54.7 million to $31.3 million, helping to improve the outlook for both Q4 and all of 2025.

This is the other positive perspective: the 2025 projection increased, with a sales forecast of $6.45 billion–$6.48 billion, while the previous guidance was $6.33 billion–$6.41 billion. The estimated EPS for 2025 was also adjusted upwards, from $1.80–$2.20 to $2.40–$2.65. The estimates took into account the tariff impact, which also improved: it went from a $100 million impact to $90 million.

VSCO EPS has been highly volatile in its quarterly variations over the past few years, but starting in 2023, and as a consequence of rising interest rates, EPS experienced several negative quarters. 2025, a tariff year, gave us a Q1 of -$0.02, a Q2 with $0.20, and now, Q3, which, although it was a smaller loss, still showed a loss: -$0.27. In the next chart you can see that trend, without counting the recent Q3.

Consumer confidence is at rock bottom

When I think about consolidated brands in cyclical consumption sectors, I also think about the environment. I ask myself, which could be the internal catalysts of the sector, in general, that could prompt such a share? And if there isn't one, could the share have, anyway, an attractive upside? Or am I only aiming to safeguard value? These are the kinds of questions I ask myself before choosing what to do with a consumer share.

To answer the question of if VSCO could have an internal catalyst that outperforms the sector and pushes the market upward, we just have to take a look at its peers. VSCO has a market cap of $3.37 billion, is present in 70 countries, and bills around $6.3 billion annually. The business is robust, but, in my opinion, as we saw in Q3, it can't move its entire sector. It is not Nvidia (NVDA) for SPY or Amazon (AMZN) for e-commerce. The players who rank at the top of their category are Industria Del Diseño Textil (OTCPK:IDEXY), The TJX Companies (TJX), and Fast Retailing (OTCPK:FRCOY), with market caps of $178 billion, $168 billion, and $110 billion, respectively.

But I still consider VSCO as a consolidated brand in the women's clothing market, especially in women's underwear. Let's make something clear: I'm not comparing VSCO with luxury brands, but I'm thinking of similar brands: American Eagle Outfitters (AEO), Phillips-Van Heusen (PVH), or Hanesbrands, now owned by Gildan Activewear (GIL:CA).

So, going back to the first question, I think in these sectors the catalysts play a key role, and therefore, economic activity in general also plays a role. But I focused on the user first, and I found a negative datum: in November, the consumer confidence index fell 6.8 points monthly to 88.7, the lowest figure since April. I found an even lower figure in 2021, when the market was still uncertain about the recovery of consumption due to the closures during the second year of the pandemic.

But another piece of data that worried me was the expectations index, which fell 8.6 points to 63.2 points. This is also the worst figure since April, but to find that same level of pessimism, I had to go back to 2013. This indicator has been below 80 points for 10 consecutive months, which, according to the Conference Board, is a sign that a recession is coming.

The impact of tariffs, inflation, and unemployment are some of the main concerns of consumers, and I think that's understandable. The first two have an impact on the price, which causes a decline in confidence due to higher prices, and the third implies that unemployment will generate less consumption.

Tariffs and inflation: Weak consumer outlook

I have seen the impact of tariffs and inflation on the outlook for holiday retail sales, a time that represents a great opportunity for consumer companies to strengthen their balance sheets. For example, Christmas sales represented 34% of VSCO in Q4 2024. However, the prospects are not the best, following the line I showed about consumer confidence.

I found this in the interesting surveys of the S&P Global report, where 84% of those surveyed estimate that their inventory will be between 1% and 10% higher than last year. This large majority demonstrates that price increases will have a significant impact on retail sales during the Christmas holidays, which are already underway, potentially leading to weak consumer spending. In fact, the sales growth estimate for this year's holidays is 4%, the same as last year, but quite below the average 5.3% of the last 10 years.

It is reasonable to think that, if consumer confidence drops and prices and unemployment are rising, consumption will suffer more pressures. But within general consumption, of course there are many differences, because consumption in restaurants or dollar stores is not the same as clothing or specialty retail. The outlook for VSCO is stable for its development in the short term, both in terms of consumption and credit performance among the 46 analyzed by S&P Global.

However, the clothing and specialty retail sector is the most challenging in terms of credit ratings during 2025. Both sectors are at the bottom of the table, as outlined in the chart below.

In this environment I see the trend of weaker consumption going on at least until 2026. It is true that the prospect of a faster rate cut in 2026 thanks to the change in leadership at the Fed could boost consumption, but in scenarios where inflation rises again, doubts about interest rates will resurface.

S&P Global also revealed some estimates for the coming years, and consumption doesn't look good. The consumption growth will be 2% for 2026 and 1.8% for 2027, and it will only return to a slight recovery in 2028 with 2%. These data are consistent with the GDP growth estimate of 2% in 2026. Under these parameters, I envision a challenging scenario for VSCO.

Fashion, a challenging sector

Beyond the difficulties of the sector and the macroeconomic context, the fashion business became a challenging career over the last years. What I have found in a study published by McKinsey is that the pessimistic trend will go on for three main reasons: tariff turbulence, changes in consumer priorities (customers are more selective, even choosing second-tier brands), and technological disruption.

Among the respondents in the study, 46% expect worse conditions for 2026 compared with the 39% expected for 2025. My opinion is that the tariff effect still generates some pessimism in the sector, and negotiations with China, although there is an agreement, will remain uncertain in 2026. As an example, I can use what I showed earlier: holiday inventory prices could increase by up to 10% compared to the previous year.

For its part, something positive looking into the future is the promise of the Chinese market and its interesting role as a potential consumption catalyst. While in 2025 41% of the sector defined China as an "unpromising" market, now only 28% define it that way for 2026. Anyway, China only represents 5% of VSCO's total revenue.

There is another difference that reduces the company's potential in the sector, since, being dedicated to fashion retail, the expectations are not the best when compared to the luxury segment. In the following chart you can see how the US market will move in a horizontal V shape (according to the two dashed lines), showing the most pessimistic scenario. The best scenario will be a growth level similar to 2025.

The environment does not favor upside potential

To understand the real value of the company, I've made two DCF models. I did the first one with a perspective based on the consensus I showed before. In other words, with a perpetual growth rate of 2%, below projected inflation.

In line with these estimations, I applied a 3% decrease for 2027 but projected a recovery from then on of 1.5%, 4%, and 6.5% for 2028, 2029, and 2030, respectively. I have used the 23% 2024 tax rate, the 7.6% WACC, and the EV/EBITDA you can find in the Valuation section of Seeking Alpha of 11.1x. In this first model, which I consider a neutral one based on the expected consensus, there is no margin for the share to grow. Please note that the company's fiscal year ends at the end of February.

For the second model, I have applied an estimated growth on a scenario where VSCO would become more resilient to the difficult environment of the sector and where it would avoid losses. For this second model, I applied a 5-year CAGR rate of 4.25% to EBIT, almost double the consensus estimate applied in the first model. In this more optimistic scenario, the upside is near 2%.

To get a more attractive upside, I should elaborate on a third model with more aggressive growth, but I think that would include a more optimistic catalyst, which I consider unlikely. At these prices and with this upside of 2% estimated in an optimistic but realistic model, I believe that holding is the best option.

Risks

The risks of the company are somehow connected to the environment I detailed before (the consumption estimates and the pessimism in the sector). The clothing retail sector has one of the worst credit ratings compared to other mass consumption sectors. The whole macroeconomic conjuncture is challenging for the company, especially the tariff effect and, as a consequence, the rise in prices.

Another risk related to the fashion industry itself is the change in consumer habits, which are becoming increasingly selective, both in terms of details and quality, and mainly in terms of convenience and the priority given to more affordable prices. These are two key points. The convenience of online shopping is an alternative that major e-commerce players constantly offer, while cheaper options, despite being thrift brands, continue to gain popularity.

Finally, the risk of not overcoming the challenges of the sector is not only a matter of lower margins, but it's also a test of the financial strength of the company. Debt management is healthy, but if the Fed's headwinds turn again with interest rates not falling at the expected pace, the costs of surviving lean times could be much higher.

Summary

In summary, I think VSCO, like much of the fashion retail clothing sector, faces structural challenges. This doesn't mean the company lacks solid balances or faces financial difficulties. I'm not saying that, nor did I mention something similar in this article. But until we see a path of firm cuts in interest rates, I do see a very complex environment. Given the limited upside provided by the valuation models I presented, I rate the company as a Hold, until stronger catalysts or lower prices emerge.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Why Tech's Comeback And A 'Hawkish' Fed Could Change Everything
data.description:
By Ryan J. Puplava

Key Points This Week:

  • Tech and Mega-Cap Leadership: After Monday’s crypto-driven selloff, mega-cap technology rebounded strongly midweek, led by NVIDIA (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), and Intel (INTC), with earnings beats and AI-driven initiatives supporting the rally.
  • Broader Market Participation: Market breadth improved as industrials, energy, financials, and consumer cyclicals posted gains, highlighting that the rally is no longer solely dependent on tech. Small caps also showed strength, signaling renewed risk appetite.
  • Fed Policy and Macro Outlook: Mixed economic data—weak manufacturing, resilient services, and sticky inflation—sets the stage for a widely expected “hawkish” Fed rate cut next week, with implications for rates, bonds, precious metals, and risk assets.
  • Chart of the Week – Silver: A bearish momentum divergence in this chart signals potential near-term caution. Strong year-to-date performance and sensitivity to Fed policy highlight the importance of monitoring technical signals alongside macro developments.

The first week of December brought a mix of volatility, sector rotation, and cautious optimism as markets navigated crypto-driven selloffs, powerful rebounds in mega-cap technology, and a full slate of economic data ahead of next week’s pivotal FOMC decision. Despite choppy day-to-day action, the S&P 500, Nasdaq, and Dow all ended the week with modest gains, reflecting underlying strength as investors brace for the policy decision.

Tech and Mega-Cap Reassert Leadership

After Monday’s crypto-related selloff pressured sentiment across risk assets, mega-cap technology regained its footing and ultimately led the market higher through midweek. Monday’s session opened sharply lower as Bitcoin and Ethereum plunged, dragging crypto-exposed equities such as Coinbase, Robinhood, and Bitmine to steep losses. Yet the broader tech complex—initially hit with early declines—showed impressive resilience. NVIDIA surged after announcing a strategic partnership with Synopsys (SNPS) to accelerate agentic AI engineering, while Apple contributed supportive gains that helped tech nearly erase its early losses.

Tuesday’s trading extended that recovery, with tech and mega-caps firmly back in the driver’s seat. Intel led the sector with an 8.7% gain following upbeat commentary on future production and free cash flow expectations, helping push the PHLX Semiconductor Index nearly 2% higher. NVIDIA, Apple, Microsoft, and Meta (META) all posted gains, and stronger-than-expected results from MongoDB (MDB) and Credo (CRDO) added fuel to the sector’s momentum.

Wednesday flipped the script as mega-cap tech finally paused, led by a pullback in Microsoft following mixed headlines around AI sales targets. Still, the broader market held its ground, reinforcing that the rally is no longer solely reliant on the largest tech names. By Thursday and Friday, the group regained footing: NVIDIA resumed its climb, Salesforce delivered an earnings beat, and although chipmakers faced late-week pressure after two senators introduced the SAFE Chips Act—legislation aimed at preventing the White House from easing export controls without Congressional approval—the sector’s overall leadership trend remained intact.

Across the week, mega-cap performance was choppy but constructive, with several sessions showing that the market can advance even when the largest stocks are mixed. This marks an important improvement from last month, when tech weakness often derailed the broader indices.

Investment Implications:

AI-driven capex, semiconductor strength, and software demand continue to be long-term tailwinds despite near-term valuation concerns. We believe positioning remains constructive for high-quality mega-cap names with strong free cash flow and visibility in AI, cloud infrastructure, and digital services. Select semiconductors and software firms with strong earnings revisions may be key overweight candidates, though volatility may increase as regulatory tensions with China re-emerge.

Please note: Investment Implications are meant as general market commentary and are not individualized investment advice or recommendations. Appropriate allocations depend on each investor’s specific objectives, financial situation, and risk tolerance.

Broader Market Participation Improves

One of the most notable developments this week was a meaningful improvement in market breadth. After Monday’s retreat—which saw utilities, health care, industrials, and financials all move lower—breadth turned sharply positive midweek. On Wednesday, advancers exceeded decliners by roughly 5-to-2 on both the NYSE and Nasdaq, and nine of eleven sectors closed higher. Importantly, this advance occurred even as tech lagged, highlighting stronger underlying support across the market.

Industrials were a consistent bright spot. Boeing soared more than 10% on Tuesday after projecting rising aircraft deliveries and positive free cash flow expectations heading into FY26. Defense names were pressured Monday by speculation surrounding a potential shift in U.S. foreign policy, but industrial cyclicals broadly regained strength through the week. Transportation, machinery, and multi-industrials posted steady gains as expectations for a December rate cut held firm.

Energy also delivered strong performance, with the sector leading on multiple days. Crude settled near $59–$60 per barrel following OPEC+’s decision to keep output steady for early 2026. Natural gas prices surged to levels not seen since 2022, lifting sentiment across exploration and production names. Even with volatility in oil prices tied to macro uncertainty, the energy complex remained a net contributor to market gains.

Consumer-facing cyclicals provided additional support. Homebuilders rallied sharply midweek as falling long-term yields and persistent expectations for Fed easing boosted rate-sensitive housing demand indicators. The SPDR S&P Homebuilders ETF (XHB) rallied back toward October highs. Retail stocks benefited from solid earnings at American Eagle (AEO), Dollar Tree (DLTR), and Ulta Beauty (ULTA), demonstrating strength even as discretionary mega-caps like Amazon fluctuated.

Financials were another standout. Bank stocks posted firm gains Wednesday, and crypto-linked financials rebounded sharply as Bitcoin recovered above $93,000. The Russell 2000, a key barometer of domestic economic expectations, outperformed on both Wednesday and Thursday, an encouraging sign that small-cap risk appetite is returning.

Investment Implications:

The expansion of market leadership beyond tech is a constructive signal for sustaining the rally into year-end. Cyclical exposures (industrials, select financials, energy, and housing-related names) stand to benefit from stabilizing macro conditions and a potential Fed cut. Investors may consider selectively increasing allocation to sectors tied to economic acceleration, particularly industrials and financials, while treating energy as a tactical overweight given renewed price stability.

“Hawkish” Fed Cut Next Week?

The week concluded with a heavy flow of economic data that largely aligned with expectations but highlighted a mixed macro landscape as the Federal Reserve prepares for its December policy decision.

Manufacturing indicators remained weak. The ISM Manufacturing Index fell to 48.2, marking the ninth consecutive month below the 50 threshold. The report’s characterization of conditions as “stagflationary” (declining output, rising prices, and weakening employment) underscored persistent challenges in the goods-producing economy. S&P Global’s manufacturing PMI also dipped modestly.

Labor market readings sent conflicting signals. ADP data showed a surprising 32,000 decline in private payrolls, raising concern about slowing hiring momentum. However, weekly initial claims dropped to their lowest level in nearly two years, reinforcing the view that the labor market remains resilient even as it gradually cools.

Inflation data was broadly in line with expectations. Core PCE, the Fed’s preferred inflation gauge, came in at 0.2% for September, unchanged from the prior month. But underlying components remained sticky and well above the Fed’s 2% target. This stickiness, coupled with solid consumer spending, supports the narrative that while the economy is slowing, it is not collapsing.

Services activity (responsible for roughly 70% of U.S. economic output) remained in expansion territory. The ISM Services Index held at 52.6, consistent with stable but slow growth. Consumer sentiment improved marginally but remained depressed by high prices and economic uncertainty.

All told, the data keeps the Fed firmly on track for a widely expected rate cut next week. However, policymakers are likely to pair the cut with cautionary language designed to prevent financial conditions from easing too quickly—hence the prevailing expectation of a “hawkish cut.” Markets now see a December cut as near certain, while odds of an additional move in January remain low.

Investment Implications:

We expect continued support for equities as easing financial conditions take hold, particularly in rate-sensitive areas such as homebuilders, small caps, and financials. However, sticky inflation and potentially a cautious Fed stance (if it happens) would suggest that yields may not fall rapidly and possibly rise if investors think the Fed will end its current path for lower rates. Earlier this week, Jonathan Krinsky pointed out that while Japan is important to watch for global yields, watching German and French yields nearing breakout levels could be an indication that bond values are in for some weakness. Especially as “yields have moved ‘higher’ immediately following the last five Fed rate cut (cycles).”

Chart of the Week

Writing this section of my weekly wrap-up is always a challenge. I could easily fill an entire article with a dozen charts. For an enthusiast, sticking to one topic is like asking a chef to describe a single spice in a dish without wandering into the entire recipe—it’s all connected, and the excitement naturally spills over. That’s how it feels as a market technician: one chart often leads to another, and the bigger picture constantly wants to break in.

This week, I’m going to resist that impulse and focus on a single setup: a bearish momentum divergence on a particular security. When I worked as a portfolio manager and market technician at Financial Sense Wealth Management, I often played a game with the investment team called “Would you buy this security?” I would hide the name and price to focus purely on the chart and technical indicators, divorcing the analysis from fundamentals—which the chart already discounts.

So… would you buy this chart now?

On this week’s chart, we see a notable momentum divergence: the 14-day RSI hit a lower peak even as price made a new high relative to October. This setup is typically a warning sign, often preceding a correction or a consolidation phase. I explored this concept in depth back in April 2011 in this article on parabolic moves and momentum divergence.

If you haven’t guessed the chart by now, it’s the continuous spot price of silver. While I don’t have space to go into full detail, the main takeaway is clear: silver has been one of the top-performing assets this year but is showing some concerning bearish momentum signals. From a fundamental perspective, a hawkish pause from the Fed next week could boost the dollar and rates, putting pressure on precious metals. That’s why I see next week’s Fed decision as especially important—not just for bond investors monitoring rate trends or currency traders watching the dollar, but also for precious metals and other risk assets that have rallied over the past two weeks on renewed hopes of another rate cut.

Conclusion

The broader market continues to balance resilient economic data against a shifting policy landscape, while leadership narrows around a handful of durable themes—AI investment, infrastructure build-out, and selective strength in consumers and credit. Technical conditions remain constructive but not without pockets of fragility that merit attention as we move into year-end positioning. As always, the path forward will be shaped by a mix of earnings quality, inflation progress, and the market’s reaction to the “Fed Speak” next week and any color the chairman can provide on the glide path for rate cuts.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: PTY: Efficient Portfolio Strategy Results In Outperformance Against Peers
data.description:
Overview

As interest rates remain high, there is a lot of uncertainty within the debt markets. However, there are plenty of high-quality opportunities out there that investors can take advantage of. I believe that PIMCO Corporate and Income Opportunity Fund (PTY) is one of the best opportunities in the high-yield debt investment sector right now, due to the attractive valuation and conservative risk profile. When I previously covered PTY, I issued a buy rating due to the potential for growth as interest rates decline.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that PTY's share price has declined by about 10.8%. This fund has continued to trend downward since the momentum shift in mid-October. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return still sits at a loss of 1.1% over the same time frame. Following the pullback, PTY now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 10.8% and issues payouts on a monthly basis, which makes it an enticing choice for investors seeking a source of reliable income generation.

Since my last coverage, PTY's valuation remains attractive and justifies the accumulation of shares. However, I also wanted to revisit the fund to discuss how it is one of the best-performing PIMCO funds at this time. Compared to relevant peers, PTY's portfolio strategy utilizes a reasonable amount of leverage that has led to higher returns. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that PTY implements to generate its earnings for shareholders.

Fund Strategy

According to the fund's latest overview, PTY has total managed assets of $2.96B that are spread across a diverse range of income producing securities. The fund implements a dynamic strategy that spreads its assets across securities with varying duration, credit quality, and sectors. Under normal market conditions, PTY aims to invest at least 80% of its assets into a combination of debt obligations and other corporate income producing securities. The fund leans most heavily on high-yield credit, but the full breakdown of the portfolio is as follows:

  • High Yield Credit: 37.96%
  • Non-USD Developed: 15.12%
  • Emerging Markets: 13.67%
  • Non-Agency Mortgage: 13.41%
  • Investment Grade Credit: 6.73%
  • Other: 4.78%
  • US Government Related: 4.12%
  • CMBS: 1.6%

Expanding on this diversity, PTY also spreads its assets across a diverse range of industries to help avoid any sort of concentration risk to any one specific area. As we can see below, PTY is most heavily allocated to Technology investments at 12.91% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to Media Cable and Healthcare securities, which accounts for 6.07% and 5.02% of assets, respectively. While the fund leans most heavily to U.S.-based securities, PTY also provides a bit of exposure to global securities spanning across France, Spain, Brazil, Germany, and more.

Since PTY is structured as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. As a result, PTY now trades at a premium to NAV of 10.75%. For reference, PTY has traded at an average premium to NAV of 23.1% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that PTY currently trades at one of the most favorable price-to-NAV levels over the last decade, which may now justify the accumulation of shares.

Performance Risk & Comparison

Despite the fund's diverse exposure, investors need to remember that PTY prioritizes income generation above all else. PTY holds its securities through different income securities rather than common equity. To demonstrate this vulnerability, we can refer to the performance chart over the last ten year period below. As we can see, PTY's share price has declined by about 4.4% over the last ten years, which makes it clear that PTY has missed out on many of the market rallies over the last decade. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 161% over the same time. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of the returns will come from the fund's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions over time.

Additionally, PTY utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify returns over time. The use of leverage is captured through the combination of reverse repurchase agreements and credit default swaps, which amounts to 18.19% of total assets. I consider this to be a very moderate use of leverage, which shouldn't significantly increase the risk profile of the fund. However, leverage still has some impact on the fund's operating spread between the cost of its debt and the yield collected from its securities.

So despite the Fed initiating its second 25 basis point cut to interest rates, the federal funds rate remains near the higher end of its ten year range. As we can see below, this higher interest rate environment has contributed to PTY's share price remaining suppressed in recent years. I anticipate that PTY's share price may eventually maintain an uptrend once interest rates continue to trend downward.

PIMCO has a plethora of funds under their umbrella that its investors can choose from. While their underlying strategy in terms of sector focus, security type, and risk profile may differ, the underlying premise of generating income from a pool of debt securities remains the same. I believe that the two most compelling peers for investors to choose from are the following:

  • PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI): ~14.6% dividend yield.
  • PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund (PCN): ~10.7% dividend yield.

As we can see below, PCN has the longest operating history, with an inception dating back to 2001, but this fund has the smallest assets under management at $848M. Conversely, PDI has the shortest operating history but a much larger base of assets under management at $7.2B. PCN is probably the most similar to PTY, because the underlying portfolio strategy also focuses on high yield credit securities and other income producing securities. Conversely, PDI leans more heavily on mortgage-related securities to generate its earnings.

When comparing the performance of the peer group, PTY outperforms these alternatives by a wide margin of performance. Over the last ten years, PTY's total return sits above 161%, which outpaces the total return of PDI and PCN over the same time frame. The outperformance can be attributed to the breakdown of securities by credit rating and the different use of leverage. PTY's leverage is quite moderate, whereas PDI's use of leverage is much higher, while PCN uses a very little amount of leverage to amplify its earnings. Therefore, I believe PTY is the most appealing choice for long-term investors out of this peer group.

The standout feature of PTY is that its breakdown by credit ratings differs from its peers. PTY allocates a larger percentage of its assets towards securities with higher credit ratings. For reference, securities with a credit rating of BBB and above indicate strength and reduce the chance that PTY is impacted by defaults. The breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 57.6%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 33.7%
  • Not Rated: 8.71%

Dividend

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1188 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 10.8%. Looking at the dividend history below, we can see that PTY has maintained a long consistency of steady payouts over the last decade. The distributions were cut back in mid-2021, but this may be attributed to the uncertainty of the pandemic at the time. Additionally, the Fed cut interest rates to near zero levels, so the income received from a portion of PTY's investments may have decreased. Despite these challenges, the fund has kept payouts within a tight range, which makes PTY a great option for investors seeking a reliable source of dividend income.

Unfortunately, I wouldn't anticipate that PTY will provide any sort of traditional dividend growth over time. However, the fund is already providing a double-digit yield, so investors should limit their expectations around dividend growth. The good thing about the consistent payouts is that this allows investors to create their own dividend growth by reinvesting distributions or adding new capital to their position over time.

However, investors should consider the fact that the distributions received from PTY do not offer tax efficiency. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that 73.14% of the distribution has been paid out using net investment income, which is classified as ordinary dividends and has the highest tax burden on investors. Therefore, a position in PTY would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset any of the potential tax consequences.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on PTY due to the attractive valuation and efficient portfolio strategy. The fund now trades at a favorable premium to NAV valuation, relative to its historical range. Additionally, PTY's portfolio strategy of focusing on higher-rated securities while utilizing a moderate amount of leverage has resulted in outperformance against other PIMCO peers. In a scenario where interest rates continue to trend downward, PTY's share price may trend upward as the operating spread between the cost of its leverage and the yield collected from securities may improve.

data.title: Canada Labor Market Watch: Unemployment Rate Drops To 6.5%
data.description:
Canada’s unemployment rate dropped substantially and unexpectedly to 6.5% in November, from 6.9% in the prior month, showing signs of a much-needed resiliency in the North American economy.

Latest Data

Metric November 2025 Prior Month (Oct 2025)
Unemployment Rate 6.5% 6.9%
Total Employment Change +53,600 +66,600
Part-Time Employment Change +63,000 +85,100
Full-Time Employment Change -9,400 -18,500
Job Finding Rate 19.6% 19.8%

Surprise labour market recovery

A 40 basis point (bps) drop in the unemployment rate is substantial. An improvement of this magnitude was last seen in November 2005, outside the period of COVID distortions seen between 2020 and mid-2022.

The strengthening of the labor market is best illustrated by the employment growth readings, which now show three consecutive months of job growth. In November, Canada recorded a gain of 54,000 jobs, following increases of 60,400 and 66,600 in September and October, respectively.

The Part-Time/Full-Time Divergence

However, the details offered some caution. For two months in a row all the employment increases came from part-time jobs (63,000 in November and 85,100 in October). If we look at full-time jobs, a combined 27,900 jobs were lost in the last two months.

The StatCan in its press release, provided some context to ease some of the worries that the recent surge in part-time employment is a sign of economic distress. The percentage of part-time employment deemed “involuntary,” defined as the decision to work part-time due to economic or business conditions or an inability to find full-time work, was 17.9% in November.

  • That is just slightly higher than the 17.6% reading in Nov 2024; and,
  • lower than the average for the Novembers from 2017 to 2019 (19.3%).

Another important figure StatCan emphasized is the job-finding rate, which was 19.6% in November, which was higher than the 18.6% a year earlier and similar to the figure reported in September (19.8%).

Health care is the biggest job creator

In terms of industry breakdown, health care and social assistance is the biggest job creator in November, adding 45,500 jobs in the month, a rebound from October’s 7,200 drop.

Wholesale and retail trade, on the other hand, lost 34,100 jobs after it gained 40,700 in the previous month. Manufacturing also cut 9,300 workers after a combined 36,500 job creation in the previous two months.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Oscar Health: Banking On The ACA Enrollment Expansion (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction and Investment Thesis

I am reviewing my previous hold rating on Oscar Health, Inc. (OSCR) to a buy rating driven by recent Affordable Care Act (ACA) enrollment expansion to Alabama and Southern Florida, among other areas. This expansion shows foresight in scaling membership while the economics around health insurance remain tight. In my previous article, I stated that amid steep insurance budget cuts, the total projected membership by 2027 was around 20 million members. Following the ACA enrollment expansion, the projected membership has now gone up to 24 million subscribers without subsidies and around 31 million subscribers with subsidies.

Already, the current enrollment outlook is without subsidies in the picture, after the deal ending the U.S.'s most extended shutdown. The deal ending the unprecedented shutdown in the history of the U.S. did not include an extension to tax credits that cushion the cost of accessing ACA Marketplaces. Despite this hiccup, the expansion seems to be the driver of the optimism of the YTD price return of 26.93%, beating the S&P 500 price return at 15.83%.

I will dive deeper into ACA enrollment expansion, how it positions the company, and why this optimism warrants a bullish outlook.

ACA Enrollment Expansion Growth Outlook

In my previous article, I highlighted the looming uncertainty from healthcare budget cuts in 2026, which is about 30 days away. From what I see, this uncertainty is even clearer given the shutdown deal that did not provide a clear path to the expiring federal tax credit that offsets some costs on ACA plans. The ACA tax credits/subsidies are set to end by 2025, which means this may affect the number of subscribers under the Advance Premium Tax Credit (APTC). This is because the ACA is directly tied to APTC, and the failure to extend these benefits after the shutdown for the year 2026 will result in lowered premiums, which will call for subscribers to go out-of-pocket to meet these costs.

Over the years, the ACA enrollment has been increasing significantly due to subsidies. For example, the current subscribers under APTC stand at around 22.4 million, from 19.7 million in 2024, representing a 13.7% increase. Compared to the number of consumers without APTC, which stands at 1.9 million, compared to 1.7 million consumers in 2024, this is a small percentage of the total consumers. There is growth, but it indicates just how the subsidies are key to ACA enrollment.

Now, when I look at the difference between consumers with APTC, it is easier to understand why it has been a primary issue resulting in the longest shutdown in the history of the U.S. As such, President Donald Trump has expressed intentions of extending ACA subsidies, terming them as necessary to cushion Americans facing their premiums skyrocketing. It is also rumored that even though ACA subsidies are set to end by 31st December 2025, lawmakers are looking at 30th January 2026 as the real end of healthcare subsidies. From where I sit, my view is that since there could be another shutdown after 30th January 2026 due to an end to healthcare subsidies, there are high chances of an extension of subsidies for three more years, even if it might require some Americans to dig deeper into their pockets.

From these external headwinds facing ACA enrollment, let’s get to Oscar Health. Despite these uncertainties, Oscar Health is still extending its reach, and from what I see, Oscar is best positioned amidst this uncertainty. In a scenario without subsidies, the company projects to have access to a total market size enrollment of around 24 million members by 2027 and 31 million members with subsidies during the same year.

This is why the expansion is crucial at this uncertainty; even without subsidies, Oscar Health has a chance to stay afloat in membership due to expansion into new metropolitan regions and innovative offerings such as small-group and ICHRA coverage. The areas like Arizona, San Antonio, New Jersey, Alabama, among others, have expanded to have a high enrollment percentage of between 100% and 200% in 5 years.

For example, in Alabama, which is within 100% to 200%, has recorded enrollment growth of 198%, from 160,429 members in 2020 to 477,838 members in 2025. Therefore, by extending to regions recording high enrollment percentages, this positions Oscar Health for more enrollment opportunities from these areas, offering ICHRA and small-group coverage. Still, taking Alabama as my pragmatic example, Alabama is also expecting to see a rise in insurance costs, which could double. Alabama Public Education Employees Health Insurance Plan (PEEHIP) is planning to request a raise of around $380 million from the legislature in an attempt to cushion employees’ plan coverage for over 104,000 people covered under its scheme.

Such actions are positioning Oscar Health to capture more memberships with its affordable health plans, such as an annual bronze deductible, which is $4,548, way cheaper than the average, which is around $5,523. On average, a 21-year-old is paying for monthly health insurance of $445, and compared to Oscar Health at $410, expansion to new areas expands market reach for potential customers in need of affordable coverage.

Financials and Valuation

The revenue performance is also one of the reasons I maintain that Oscar has foresight. The long-term strategy, such as scalable operations, is the primary driver of the projected CAGR of revenue at 20% to 2027. So far, the company is engaging in these activities in expansion while offering disruptive products such as ICHRA. Now, when you add all that together, the company has kept a 20% revenue growth YoY commitment. The revenue in the last quarter rose by 23.21%, from $2,864 million in Q3’2024 to $2,986 million in Q3’2025.

So far, the company has reaffirmed 2025 revenue guidance of between $12.0 billion and $12.2 billion. This means that in terms of annual revenue growth, FY2025 is likely to increase by around 31.52%, which is within the consensus revenue estimate of $12.02 billion. With the expansion discussed above, which aims to have a total market reach of about 16 million subscribers from the expected 6 million subscribers between 2025 and 2027, the revenues are set to increase beyond 2025.

Even though the company is not yet profitable at the moment, with a trailing net loss of $244.09 million, it is expected, due to huge expenses on expansion commitments, to scale its revenue base. From FY2022, the company has demonstrated strong execution by reducing net loss from $606.3 million to a net profit of $25.4 million in 2024. With the expansion in 2025, which seems to have resulted in investing back its earnings, the earnings projection shows impressive possible returns, as shown by the upward consensus estimates. A good example of this investing back into the company is seen in the projected Q4’2025 earnings miss by around 47.72% YoY, followed by an optimistic Q2’2026 at 93.26% earnings rebound.

To wrap up this section, I will value Oscar Health using the P/S ratio compared to that of its competitors, Humana Inc. (NYSE:HUM) and Centene Corporation (NYSE:CNC). Oscar Health seems to be trading at a premium relative to its competitors at a P/S ratio of 0.37x, which is a distant from HUM’s P/S ratio of 0.23x and CNC’s at 0.11x. I think this is a justified market valuation given expansion efforts to capture the market through more enrollment. This is because while FY2026 faces significant subsidy uncertainty as discussed above, the company still has a revenue growth rate estimate of 4.77%, compared to HUM, which is -27.56%. CNS may seem to have a high revenue growth rebound in 2026 of 46.33%, but it is after a -72.14% projected revenue drop in 2025. Therefore, Oscar Health is relatively stable compared to its peers following expansion commitments, new products such as ICHRA that are already commercialised, and affordable policy charges.

Investment Risks

Regulatory and Policy Uncertainty

Oscar Health faces a heavy risk of ACA subsidy uncertainties. The company is subject to federal regulations, such as potential subsidies modification or expiration post 31st December 2025, which could materially impact membership growth and revenue. The ACA expansion exposes the company to a shifting regulatory and political environment. For example, if subsidies are reduced or withdrawn, the affordability of Oscar’s policy charges may erode and may affect enrollment and profitability. Possible increases without subsidies already indicate a significant increase in insurance policies, which might result in possible membership attrition.

Medical Cost Volatility and Margin Pressure

Right now, when the subsidies are still effective, the medical loss ratio as of Q3’2025 is at 88.5%, from 84.6% in 2024. Following FY2025's reaffirmed guidance of a medical loss ratio ranging between 86.0% and 87.0%, it indicates that the company is still facing cost pressure. With the modifications or withdrawal of subsidies as explained above, the company is likely to encounter sustained cost pressures. As such, more dollars may need to be allocated to cover medical expenses. This may eventually result in a challenge in meeting financial forecasts.

Conclusion

I am rerating OSCR from a hold to a buy, a bullish stance on Oscar Health, driven by ACA expansion enrollment to new areas. I believe that this fundamental optimism, especially in regions with high enrollment scores, is a significant step forward to consolidate more members, which has a direct impact on revenues.
